questions.scenario: A platform team requests one AWS subnet for a bursty worker pool. They need at least 3,900 assignable IPv4 addresses during peak deploys. IPAM has these free aligned blocks in the target VPC: `/22`, `/21`, `/20`, and `/19`. AWS reserves 5 addresses in every subnet. Which is the smallest block that satisfies the request?

options.text: `/22`, because it has 1,024 total addresses and reserves do not apply to private subnets
`/21`, because 2,048 total addresses leaves enough room after AWS reservations
`/20`, because 4,096 total addresses leaves 4,091 usable after AWS reserves
`/19`, because every subnet with more than 1,000 hosts must use 8,192 addresses

questions.explanation: The article gives the formula `2^(32 - prefix)` total addresses and notes AWS reserves 5 per subnet. A `/20` has 4,096 total and 4,091 usable in AWS, so it is the smallest listed block that clears 3,900. `/21` and `/22` are too small. `/19` works but is not the smallest satisfying choice.

questions.scenario: During a peer review, someone writes `10.50.4.130/25` on a firewall ticket and asks for the subnet boundary before approving the rule. What are the network and broadcast addresses for this subnet?
questions.explanation: A `/25` splits a `/24` into two blocks of 128 addresses: `.0` through `.127`, and `.128` through `.255`. The address `.130` is in the second half, so the network is `10.50.4.128` and the broadcast is `10.50.4.255`. The key mental model is that subnet boundaries align to the block size.

questions.scenario:
A VPC plan starts with `10.0.0.0/16`. Two `/20` subnets are already allocated:

```text
10.0.0.0/20
10.0.16.0/20
```

The team wants the next adjacent `/20` without overlap or gaps. Which block should IPAM allocate next?

options.text: `10.0.20.0/20`, because the previous block starts at 16
`10.0.24.0/20`, because each block advances by 8 in the third octet
`10.0.32.0/20`, because a `/20` advances by 16 in the third octet
`10.0.255.0/20`, because it is the last subnet in the `/16`

questions.explanation: A `/20` has 4,096 addresses, which is 16 full values in the third octet. The sequence inside `10.0.0.0/16` is `10.0.0.0/20`, `10.0.16.0/20`, `10.0.32.0/20`, and so on. Starting at 20 or 24 is not aligned to the `/20` boundary and would overlap an existing block or be invalid.

questions.scenario: A cloud route table has 47 entries and the soft limit is 50. A team wants to add 12 new `/24` application subnets from `10.30.0.0/16` behind the same transit gateway attachment. What allocation strategy best avoids the route limit?

options.text: Scatter the `/24` blocks across the `/16` so future teams have more choices
Allocate the `/24` blocks inside one aligned supernet, for example a `/20`, and advertise the single covering route
Use `/32` host routes so each instance can move independently
Avoid CIDR notation and write the subnet mask in dotted decimal instead

questions.explanation: The article explains route summarization: adjacent, power-of-two-aligned blocks can be advertised as one shorter prefix. A `/20` covers 16 `/24`s, enough for the 12 requested subnets plus room. Scattering the allocations prevents summarization and consumes route entries. `/32` routes make the limit problem worse.

questions.scenario: A team asks for `10.70.8.0/29` in AWS for a tiny internal load balancer tier because they only expect two resources. What is the correct review response?

options.text: Reject it, AWS VPC subnets cannot be smaller than `/28`, and AWS reserves 5 addresses in each subnet
Approve it, because `/29` has 8 addresses and 2 resources fit
Approve it only if it is a private subnet with no route to an internet gateway
Reject it only if the subnet is in `192.168.0.0/16`

questions.explanation: The VPC practice section says AWS rejects anything smaller than `/28`. Even when a tiny block looks sufficient on paper, cloud provider constraints and reserved addresses matter. The right move is to allocate at least a `/28`, and often a larger block if the tier may grow.

questions.scenario:
You are reviewing a subnet plan. Which comments are valid based on CIDR sizing and the AWS constraints covered in the article? Select all that apply.

```text
worker subnet: 10.10.0.0/22 for 1,500 instances
public edge:   10.10.8.0/28 for a small load balancer tier
data tier:     10.10.16.0/21 for 1,000 instances with growth headroom
legacy tier:   10.10.24.0/30 for one appliance ENI
```

options.text: The worker subnet is undersized because a `/22` has only 1,024 total addresses before AWS reservations
The public edge `/28` is the smallest AWS-allowed subnet size, with 11 usable addresses after reserves
The data tier `/21` has 2,048 total addresses, so it fits 1,000 instances with reasonable headroom
The legacy tier `/30` is acceptable in AWS because one ENI needs only one usable IP
Every listed subnet must be changed to `/24` because cloud VPCs cannot route mixed prefix lengths

questions.explanation: A `/22` cannot hold 1,500 instances. A `/28` is the AWS minimum and leaves 11 usable addresses after the 5 reserved addresses. A `/21` provides 2,048 total addresses, enough for 1,000 with room. A `/30` is below AWS's allowed minimum, so it should be rejected. VPCs routinely route mixed prefix lengths.

questions.scenario: A VM is configured with IP `10.0.1.50` and mask `255.255.255.0`. The actual cloud subnet is `10.0.0.0/20`. The VM can reach `10.0.1.80`, but traffic to `10.0.2.100` takes the default gateway path and intermittently fails. What is the root cause?

options.text: The VM has the wrong mask, so it thinks only `10.0.1.0/24` is local even though `10.0.2.100` is inside the real `/20`
The VM needs a public IP because private addresses cannot communicate across subnets
The broadcast address for `10.0.0.0/20` is `10.0.1.255`, so `10.0.2.100` is outside the VPC
The default gateway must be removed because gateways are never used inside a VPC

questions.explanation: The article's wrong-mask failure mode matches this exactly. The host was configured as `/24`, so it treats only `10.0.1.0` through `10.0.1.255` as local. In the actual `/20`, `10.0.2.100` is local and should be reached directly. The mismatched mask sends same-subnet traffic to the gateway and creates confusing intermittent failures.
